name: Coal Mining Basins Loop from Villablino
name_original: Toreno desde Villablino – circular por Riello – Espacio Natural de los Ancares Leoneses y Alto Sil
description: This difficult 101.6-mile road cycling tour from Villablino via Riello explores the stunning Ancares Leoneses y Alto Sil natural park.
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Villablino are set within the Laciana Valley, a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve in León, Spain. The region is characterized by dramatic mountainous terrain, featuring peaks over 2,000 meters and deep river valleys. Cyclists encounter lush mixed forests of birch, oak, and chestnut, traversed by the Sil River. This landscape offers varied gradients and scenic routes for road cyclists. Best road cycling routes around Villablino The most popular road cycling route is Coal Mining Basins Loop from Villablino, a 101.6 miles (163.5 km) trail that takes 7 hours 48 minutes to complete. This route features significant elevation gain through the region's challenging terrain. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Puerto de Leitariegos – La Chabola de Vallao loop from Parking Leitariegos, a difficult 43.8 miles (70.5 km) path. This route includes a substantial climb to the Leitariegos Pass, offering expansive mountain views. Local road cyclists also love the Church of Matarrosa del Sil – View of Villablino loop from Villager de Laciana, a 41.9 miles (67.6 km) trail leading through river valleys and offering views of Villablino, often completed in about 3 hours 5 minutes. Road cycling around Villablino is defined by mountainous terrain, lush forests, and river valleys, with routes passing through traditional villages and high mountain meadows. The network offers options for both moderate and difficult ability levels. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 270 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Villablino's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when road cycling around Villablino?
A: Road cycling around Villablino offers a diverse and challenging experience. The region, nestled in the Laciana Valley, is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve characterized by dramatic mountainous terrain with peaks exceeding 2,000 meters. You'll encounter significant elevation gains and descents, lush mixed forests of birch, oak, and chestnut, and routes that follow the Sil River through deep valleys. This varied landscape provides both demanding climbs and picturesque, scenic rides.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for different skill levels in Villablino?
A: Yes, Villablino offers road cycling routes for various skill levels. While the region is known for its challenging mountainous terrain, with 10 routes classified as &lt;strong&gt;difficult&lt;/strong&gt;, there are also 8 &lt;strong&gt;moderate&lt;/strong&gt; routes available. These moderate options allow cyclists to enjoy the stunning scenery without tackling the most extreme climbs, making the area accessible to a broader range of riders.

Q: Are there any family-friendly road cycling options in the Villablino area?
A: For families looking for an easier ride, the region features Vías Verdes (Greenways) that utilize former railway lines. The "Vía verde de Laciana" from Villablino to Caboalles de Arriba is a recommended 16-kilometer route, perfect for families and children. It passes through beautiful forests of oak, birch, and holly, offering a gentle and scenic experience.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or natural features to see along the road cycling routes?
A: The road cycling routes around Villablino are rich with natural and historical landmarks. You can climb to the significant &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5371946" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Leitariegos&lt;/a&gt;, a mountain pass at 1,520 meters, offering expansive views. The region is also home to impressive peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3338085" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Cornón&lt;/a&gt; and &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5518203" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Catoute&lt;/a&gt;. Additionally, you might encounter traditional "brañas" (high mountain meadows) and historical sites like Romanesque churches and old mining infrastructure.

Q: Can I find fully paved road cycling routes in Villablino?
A: Many of the road cycling routes in Villablino are fully paved, especially those designed for race bikes, allowing for smooth riding through the mountainous terrain and valleys. The "Bike Minera Laciana" project, which repurposes former mining railway lines, also includes sections with asphalt, providing accessible and scenic paved paths for cyclists.

Q: What is the "Bike Minera Laciana" and is it suitable for road bikes?
A: The "Bike Minera Laciana" is a significant cycling initiative in the region that transforms former mining railway lines into scenic routes. This circular path, nearly 40 kilometers long, allows cyclists to explore the area's industrial heritage alongside its natural beauty. It offers various options, including shorter 7 km and 8 km routes. While some sections may be compacted earth, many parts are asphalted, making them suitable for road bikes, particularly the dedicated Vías Verdes sections.

Q: What is the best time of year for road cycling in Villablino?
A: The best time for road cycling in Villablino is generally during the warmer months, from late spring through early autumn. During this period, the weather is more stable, and mountain passes are typically clear of snow, allowing access to the full range of routes. The lush forests are vibrant, and the high mountain meadows are at their most picturesque.

Q: Are there any challenging long-distance road cycling routes available?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a significant challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1045766142/coal-mining-basins-loop-from-villablino" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coal Mining Basins Loop from Villablino&lt;/a&gt; is a demanding 101.6-mile (163.5 km) route. This trail features substantial elevation gain through the region's challenging terrain, offering a rewarding long-distance experience.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Villablino?
A: Parking is generally available in Villablino itself and at key starting points for many routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/33725845" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puerto de Leitariegos – La Chabola de Vallao loop&lt;/a&gt; specifically mentions starting from Parking Leitariegos, indicating designated parking facilities at popular access points for mountain passes and trails.

Q: Are there any road cycling routes that offer views of Villablino?
A: Yes, several routes provide scenic views of Villablino and the surrounding valley. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20158667" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Church of Matarrosa del Sil – View of Villablino loop from Villager de Laciana&lt;/a&gt; is a 41.9-mile (67.6 km) trail that leads through river valleys and offers excellent vistas of Villablino. Another option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38276671" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of Villablino – Albergue Leitariegos loop from Villablino&lt;/a&gt;, which also features panoramic views.

Q: What kind of cultural or historical sites can I encounter while cycling?
A: The cycling routes in Villablino often intertwine with the region's rich cultural and industrial heritage. You can pass by traditional villages, Romanesque churches like San Miguel in Villablino or the 11th-century church in Robles de Laciana, and even a Roman bridge in Rioscuro. The "Bike Minera Laciana" project specifically highlights industrial archaeology sites such as the emblematic Pozo María and the old Villablino railway station.

Q: What makes the Laciana Valley a special place for road cycling?
A: The Laciana Valley is a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve, making it a truly special place for road cycling. This designation signifies its exceptional ecological value and conservation efforts. Cyclists can experience a unique blend of dramatic mountainous landscapes, lush mixed forests, traditional "brañas" (high mountain meadows), and rich biodiversity, including species like the brown bear and capercaillie, all contributing to an immersive natural experience.
tips: Surrounded by mountains, the Las Razas Reservoir is a small hydroelectric reservoir dam near Villablino that uses the waters of the Sil River to produce electricity.
Matarrosa del Sil is a small mining town on the banks of the River Sil.

Beautiful mining town in the province of León surrounded by very interesting trails and paths for those who love mountain biking. In the town we can visit the church of Tremor located next to the river or the hermitage of San Esteban

Riello is a small town in the province of León, located next to the Omaña River, in the Western Leonesa Mountains. Like many of the settlements in this mineral-rich area, it goes back to the Bronze Age and the Roman exploitation of gold.
Villablino is a coal mining and industrial city surrounded by a landscape of mountains and valleys in the northwest of the province of León.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: This route is primarily a hard road ride, characterized by mostly well-paved surfaces that are generally easy to ride. It includes about 121 km of regular road, 42.1 km of state road, and 607 meters of street. However, be aware that a portion of the route between Riello and Villablino can include forest tracks (up to 35%) which may become muddy or waterlogged after rainy periods, potentially requiring you to push your bike.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: The tour is estimated to take approximately 7 hours and 48 minutes to complete, covering a distance of about 164 kilometers with a significant elevation gain of 2,290 meters. This duration is for cyclists with a very good fitness level.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the route?
faqs.answer: The tour offers excellent views of the surrounding mountains and green valleys. Notable highlights include the Las Rozas Reservoir, the Church of Matarrosa del Sil, and the settlements of Tremor de Arriba and Riello. You'll also get a great View of Villablino.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: Given the significant elevation and potential for unpaved, muddy sections, the best time to cycle this route would generally be during the drier months, typically late spring, summer, and early autumn, to avoid adverse weather conditions and enjoy the best views of the natural park.

faqs.question: Are there any permits or entrance fees required for this tour?
faqs.answer: This cycling tour is located within the Espacio Natural de los Ancares Leoneses y Alto Sil, which is part of the Reserva de la Biosfera de Babia. Generally, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required for cycling on public roads and established routes within these natural areas. However, always respect local regulations and signage.

faqs.question: Where can I park to start this cycling tour?
faqs.answer: As this is a circular route starting from Villablino, you can typically find parking within or near the town to begin your tour. Specific trailhead parking information isn't provided, but Villablino serves as a convenient starting and ending point.

faqs.question: Is this cycling tour dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While dogs are often welcome in natural parks, this is a demanding road cycling route. Bringing a dog would depend on its ability to keep up with a long-distance bike ride, potentially on a leash alongside you, or if you have a suitable bike trailer. Always ensure your dog's safety and comfort, and check local regulations regarding dogs in the Espacio Natural de los Ancares Leoneses y Alto Sil.

faqs.question: Does this route intersect with other well-known trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates sections of several other paths. You will cycle along parts of the Ruta de las Cuencas Mineras del Carbón, the Camino Olvidado (La Robla-Cirujales), the Camino Olvidado (Cirujales-Cacabelos), and the Bike Mirena Laciana. Smaller segments also overlap with the Camino Natural La Mirada Circular, the Camino Natural Via Verde de Villablino a Vilaseca, and the Cañada La Vizana.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You'll experience the stunning, wild, and somewhat isolated landscapes of the Espacio Natural de los Ancares Leoneses y Alto Sil. Expect to see glacial and fluvial morphology-sculpted mountains, green valleys, and peaks around 2000 meters in elevation, offering excellent views of the ports. Notable peaks in the region include Cornón (2,188m) and Valdeiglesias (2,134m).

faqs.question: What should I pack for this demanding road cycling tour?
faqs.answer: Given the route's length and difficulty, you should pack essentials like plenty of water and snacks, appropriate cycling gear, a repair kit for your bike, navigation tools, sun protection, and layers of clothing suitable for changing mountain weather. Be prepared for potential unpaved sections, especially between Riello and Villablino.

faqs.question: Are there places to find food or accommodation along the route?
faqs.answer: The route passes through settlements like Tremor de Arriba and Riello, as well as starting and ending in Villablino. These towns are likely to offer options for cafes, pubs, and accommodation, especially in Villablino. It's advisable to check availability in advance, particularly in smaller villages.
